title: On Pitts' Relational Properties of Domains
abstract: Andrew Pitts' framework of relational properties of domains is a powerful method for defining predicates or relations on domains, with applications ranging from reasoning principles for program equivalence to proofs of adequacy connecting denotational and operational semantics. Its main appeal is handling recursive definitions that are not obviously well-founded: as long as the corresponding domain is also defined recursively, and its recursion pattern lines up appropriately with the definition of the relations, the framework can guarantee their existence. Pitts' original development used the Knaster-Tarski fixed-point theorem as a key ingredient. In these notes, I show how his construction can be seen as an instance of other key fixed-point theorems: the inverse limit construction, the Banach fixed-point theorem and the Kleene fixed-point theorem. The connection underscores how Pitts' construction is intimately tied to the methods for constructing the base recursive domains themselves, and also to techniques based on guarded recursion, or step-indexing, that have become popular in the last two decades.
extracted_text:
arXiv:2207.07053v1  [cs.PL]  14 Jul 2022
On Pitts’ Relational Properties of Domains
Arthur Azevedo de Amorim
July 18, 2022
Abstract
Andrew Pitts’ framework of relational properties of domains is a power-
ful method for deﬁning predicates or relations on domains, with applications
ranging from reasoning principles for program equivalence to proofs of ade-
quacy connecting denotational and operational semantics. Its main appeal is
handling recursive deﬁnitions that are not obviously well-founded: as long as
the corresponding domain is also deﬁned recursively, and its recursion pattern
lines up appropriately with the deﬁnition of the relations, the framework can
guarantee their existence.
Pitts’ original development used the Knaster-Tarski ﬁxed-point theorem as
a key ingredient. In these notes, I show how his construction can be seen as
an instance of other key ﬁxed-point theorems: the inverse limit construction,
the Banach ﬁxed-point theorem and the Kleene ﬁxed-point theorem. The con-
nection underscores how Pitts’ construction is intimately tied to the methods
for constructing the base recursive domains themselves, and also to techniques
based on guarded recursion, or step-indexing, that have become popular in the
last two decades.
1
The Original Result
When reasoning about programs, it is common to compare their behaviors. We
might ask if two programs behave equivalently, if their public outputs are equal,
or if one program terminates more often than the other, among other questions.
Many of these issues can be phrased naturally using recursive relations. For ex-
ample, to argue that two functions are equivalent, we might want to check if they
produce equivalent outputs when applied to equivalent inputs, for some notion of
equivalence. However, when reasoning about higher-order or stateful programs,
equivalence for inputs and outputs is deﬁned in terms of equivalence for arbitrary
programs. Thus, we end up with a circular deﬁnition of equivalence, which re-
quires care to justify formally without running into paradoxes.
Andrew Pitts’ framework of relational properties of domains [Pit96] is a powerful
tool for constructing such relations. We can summarize the idea as follows.
Theorem 1.1 ([Pit96]). Let 퐷be an object of a pointed 햢햯햮-category 풞. Suppose
that 퐷is equipped with an isomorphism 푖∶퐹(퐷, 퐷) ≅퐷that satisﬁes the minimal
1


invariant property, where 퐹∶풞표푝× 풞→풞is a 햢햯햮-functor. Suppose moreover that
풞is equipped with an admissible relational structure ℛ, and that 퐹acts on ℛ. Then
there exists 푅퐷∈ℛ퐷such that 푅퐷= (푖−1)∗퐹(푅퐷, 푅퐷).
Here is how we can read this result intuitively, before diving into formal deﬁ-
nitions. The object 퐷is a universe where we model the behavior of the programs.
In Pitts’ original result, 퐷was assumed to be a complete partial order, or CPO, a
domain-theoretic notion for modeling general recursionand nontermination. Here,
instead, we assume that 퐷lives in some pointed 햢햯햮-category 풞, a generalization
allows us to carry the core of Pitts’ arguments while accounting for variations that
have been explored in the literature, such as families of CPOs [AFJ20], diagrams of
CPOs [Lev02], or CPOs equipped with a metric [Aze+17].
We assume that 퐷is deﬁned recursively as 퐹(퐷, 퐷) ≅퐷. The equation is stated
using a functor 퐹, where each recursive occurrence of 퐷is either contravariant or
covariant; being a 햢햯햮-functor simply means that 퐹interacts well with the struc-
ture of 풞. In principle, there could be many solutions to such equations, but The-
orem 1.1 only applies to those that satisfy the minimal invariant property, which
roughly means that 퐷is completely characterized by repeatedly unfolding its deﬁ-
nition.
The conclusion of the theorem says that we can construct some “relation”푅퐷on
퐷. In the applications we sketched above, 푅퐷could be a binary relation on a CPO,
but the result applies to other settings as well, such as relations of diﬀerent arities
or families of relations. The relational structure ℛformalizes which properties are
required of the notion of “relation” for the construction to apply. The deﬁnition of
푅퐷is given by a recursive equation 푅퐷= (푖−1)∗퐹(푅퐷, 푅퐷), which is derived from an
action of 퐹on ℛ. Diﬀerent actions and relational structures yield diﬀerent deﬁni-
tions, and it is our job to choose them appropriately depending on the application
at hand.
Let us now spell out how this works in detail. A complete partial order (CPO)
is a poset (푋, ⊑) such that every increasing chain 푥∶ℕ
mono
,,,,,→푋has a limit, or least
upper bound, denoted lim푛푥(푛). A CPO 푋is pointed if it has a least element ⊥∈푋.
A function 푓∶푋→푌between CPOs is continuous, denoted 푓∶푋
cont
,,,,→푌, if it
is monotone and preserves limits. CPOs and continuous functions between them
form a category 햢햯햮. This category is cartesian closed; the exponential 푌푋is given
by the set of continuous functions of type 푋
cont
,,,,→푌ordered pointwise.
A 햢햯햮-category is a category 풞where the sets of morphisms 풞(푋, 푌) are CPOs,
and such that composition (−)◦(−) ∶풞(푌, 푍) × 풞(푋, 푌) →풞(푋, 푍) is contin-
uous. (Product CPOs are ordered component-wise.) The most basic example of
햢햯햮-category is 햢햯햮itself, for the order relation on continuous functions deﬁned
above. Another example is given by functor categories of the form 풞퐼, where 풞is
a 햢햯햮-category and 퐼is a small category. A morphism in 풞퐼is a family of arrows
(푋푖→푌푖)푖∈퐼, and we obtain a 햢햯햮-category by ordering such families pointwise.
Combined with the previous example, this shows that families or diagrams of CPOs
also form 햢햯햮-categories. If 풞is a 햢햯햮-category, then so is 풞표푝, by inheriting the
2


structure on 풞. A 햢햯햮-functor 퐹∶풞→풟is a functor whose action on morphisms
is a continuous function.
Given a 햢햯햮-category, we say that 푌∈풞is pointed if 풞(푋, 푌) is pointed for
every 푋, and if ⊥◦푓= ⊥for every 푓. When 풞= 햢햯햮, this deﬁnition of pointedness
coincides with the one given above. Every terminal object 1 is pointed: if 푋is an
object, the unique arrow of type 푋→1 is the least element. We say that 풞itself
is pointed if every object is pointed and it has a terminal object 1. In this case, any
⊥∶푋→푌in 풞factors through 1. For example, 햢햯햮is not a pointed 햢햯햮-category
(because not every CPO is pointed according to our deﬁnition), but we do obtain
a pointed 햢햯햮-category by restricting ourselves to pointed CPOs. (More generally,
any 햢햯햮-category 풞with a terminal object has a pointed counterpart 풞⊥obtained
by restricting 풞to pointed objects.)
Given an isomorphism 푖∶퐹(퐷, 퐷) ≅퐷in 풞, where 퐷is pointed, we say that 퐷
has the minimal invariant property if the following condition holds. First, given
휋∶퐷
cont
,,,,→퐷, we deﬁne Φ(휋) ∶퐷
cont
,,,,→퐷as Φ(휋) ≜푖◦퐹(휋, 휋)◦푖−1, and pose
휋푖≜Φ푖(⊥). Intuitively, each 휋푖is a projection function that truncates 퐷to allow
for at most 푖unfoldings of its deﬁnition; hence, lim푖휋푖is a projection function that
allows for an arbitrarynumber of unfoldings. Intuitively, we would expect lim푖휋푖to
be the identity on 퐷, because leaving the number of unfoldings unbounded should
be tantamount to not truncating 퐷at all. However, this is not necessarily true; the
best we can show in general is lim푖휋푖⊑1퐷. The minimal invariant property says
precisely that we can strengthen this inequality to lim푖휋푖= 1퐷.
Given a category 풞, a relational structure on 풞is simply a functor ℛ∶풞표푝→
햢햫햺헍∧, where 햢햫햺헍∧is the category of complete lattices and functions that preserve
greatest lower bounds. We denote the value of ℛat some object 푋∈풞as ℛ푋, and
we use the variables 푅, 푆and 푇to range over the elements of ℛ푋. If 푓∶푋→푌is a
morphism in 풞, we write ℛ(푓) as 푓∗when ℛcan be understood from the context.
Example 1.2. Our motivating example of relational structure is the one obtained
by choosing 풞= 햢햯햮, and posing ℛ푋to be the set of binary relations between
the elements of 푋ordered by inclusion. The greatest lower bound of a family of
relations is simply their intersection. And the action of a continuous function 푓∶
푋
cont
,,,,→푌on ℛ푌takes the inverse image of a relation by 푓. For intuition, we’ll keep
this vocabulary when discussing other relational structures as well.
To deﬁne admissible relational structures, it is convenient to shift our perspec-
tive a bit. Given a relational structure ℛon 풞, we can build a category, also denoted
ℛ, as follows. The objects of ℛare pairs (푋, 푅), where 푋∈풞and 푅∈ℛ푋. (By
abuse of notation, I’ll often use 푅to represent the object (푋, 푅) ∈ℛ.) A morphism
푓∶(푋, 푅) →(푌, 푆) is a morphism 푓∶푋→푌in 풞such that 푅≤푓∗푆. In terms
of Example 1.2, this simply means that the function 푓takes elements related by 푅
to elements related by 푆. We can check that identities and composition in 풞can be
lifted to the morphisms of ℛ. We have a canonical functor 푝∶ℛ→풞that maps
the object (푋, 푅) to 푋and acts as the identity on morphisms.
By unfolding deﬁnitions, we can restate some of the properties of the functor ℛ
in terms of the above construction:
3


Lemma 1.3. Let 푋, 푌and 푍be arbitrary objects of 풞.
1. 1푋∶푅→푆⟺푅≤푆, for all 푅, 푆∈ℛ푋.
2. 푓∶푓∗푆→푆for any 푆∈ℛ푌and 푓∶푋→푌.

푅∈ℛ푋.

푅∈ℛ푋and 푆∈ℛ퐼
푌.
If ℛis a relational structure over a pointed 햢햯햮-category 풞, we say that a rela-
tion 푆∈ℛ푌is admissible if the following conditions hold for all 푅∈ℛ푋. First,
⊥∶푅→푆; second, lim푖푓푖∶푅→푆whenever (푓푖∶푅→푆)푖∈ℕis an increas-
ing sequence of morphisms. We say that ℛitself is admissible if every relation is
admissible. Intuitively, being admissible means that a relation always holds of di-
verging programs and is compatible with recursive program deﬁnitions, which are
constructed using limits via Kleene’s ﬁxed point theorem.
Example 1.4. We can adapt Example 1.2 to obtain an admissible relational struc-
ture as follows. First, instead of considering arbitrary CPOs,we just consider pointed
ones; that is, we take 풞= 햢햯햮⊥. Second, instead of considering arbitrary relations,
we consider only those that contain ⊥and are closed under taking limits of chains.
The missing piece in the statement of Theorem 1.1 is what it means for a 햢햯햮-
functor 퐹∶풞표푝× 풞→풞to act on ℛ. For each 푅∈ℛ푋and 푆∈ℛ푌, we assume
that there is some 퐹(푅, 푆) ∈ℛ퐹(푋,푌); moreover, if 푓∶푅′ →푅and 푔∶푆→푆′ are
morphisms in ℛ, then 퐹(푓, 푔) should be a morphism of type 퐹(푅, 푆) →퐹(푅′, 푆′) in
ℛ. (Note the contravariance on ﬁrst argument).
We can now sketch the main idea of Pitts’ original construction. For the rest
of the paper, we ﬁx some pointed 햢햯햮-category 풞equipped with an admissible
relational structure ℛ, a 햢햯햮-functor 퐹∶풞표푝× 풞→풞with an action on ℛ,
and an object 퐷that satisﬁes the minimal invariant property for an isomorphism
푖∶퐹(퐷, 퐷) ≅퐷.
Proof of Theorem 1.1. The proof relies on the Knaster-Tarski ﬁxed point theorem:
every monotone function on a complete lattice has a least ﬁxed point. Since the
mapping 푅↦(푖−1)∗퐹(푅, 푅) is not monotone, we need to modify its deﬁnition a bit.

Intuitively, this means that the constructed relation 푅퐷still holds after we truncate
an element of 퐷after 푖unfoldings. This property, known as uniformity, will play
an important role in Section 3, when constructing 푅퐷by the Banach ﬁxed-point
theorem.
Remark 1.6. Pitts’ presentation diﬀers from mine in a few respects [Pit96]. What
I call a relational structure here corresponds to what he calls a relational structure
with inverse images and intersections. More importantly, his notion of action on
a relational structure is diﬀerent: rather than requiring ℛto be admissible, he re-
quires 퐹(푅, 푆) to be admissible whenever 푆is. This is a strengthening of the above
notion of action, since it must be deﬁned even for relations that are not admissible.
It allows us to formulate more useful coinduction principles associated with the
relation 푅퐷, but it does not change the construction of 푅퐷itself, which is why we
do not consider it here.
2
Inverse Limit Construction
In practice, minimal invariants such as 퐷are often obtained with Scott’s inverse
limit construction. The method can be seen as an adaptation of Kleene’s ﬁxed-point
theoremthat accounts for mixed-variancefunctors, and can be carriedout for many
햢햯햮-categories [Wan79; SP82]. After reviewing the idea, we will see that Pitts’ re-
sult, Theorem 1.1, is just an instance of it!
We say that two morphisms 푓푒∶푋→푌and 푓푝∶푌→푋in 풞form an
embedding-projection pair if 푓푝푓푒= 1푋and 푓푒푓푝⊑1푌. We can show that each half
of the pair uniquely determines the other. Embeddings and projections compose,
so we can form a subcategory 풞푒consisting of all embeddings, and 풞푝consisting
of all projections. With embeddings and projections, we can make mixed-variance
functors more symmetric. Since 퐹is a 햢햯햮-functor, its action on morphisms is
monotone, and we can show that 퐹(푓푝, 푓푒) is an embedding, with 퐹(푓푒, 푓푝) being
the corresponding projection. Thus, 퐹determines a functor 퐹푒∶풞푒→풞푒by
posing 퐹푒(푋) ≜퐹(푋, 푋) on objects, and 퐹푒(푓푒) ≜퐹푒(푓푝, 푓푒) on morphisms.
Much like Kleene’s ﬁxed-point theorem, we’ll see that we can build 퐷by con-
sidering a chain of ﬁnite iterations of 퐹푒and taking its colimit—which, in the con-
text of Kleene’s construction, would just correspond to a limit in a CPO. Since we
are dealing with embeddings, colimits behave particularly symmetrically, a phe-
nomenon known in the literature as the limit-colimit coincidence:
5


Theorem 2.1 ([SP82]). Let 푋푒∶(ℕ, ≤) →풞푒be a diagram of embeddings, which
uniquely corresponds to a diagram 푋푝∶(ℕ, ≥) →풞푝of projections. Let 퐴∈풞. The
following conditions are equivalent.
• 퐴is a colimit of 푋푒in 풞.
• 퐴is a limit of 푋푝in 풞.
• There is a cocone of embeddings 푓푒∶푋푒→∆퐴such that lim푖푓푒

• There is a cone of projections 푓푝∶∆퐴→푋푝such that lim푖푓푒
푖◦푓푝

In this situation, 푓푒∶푋푒→∆퐴is a colimiting cocone, and 푓푝∶∆퐴→푋푝is a
limiting cone. We call the pair (퐴, 푓) the bilimit of 푋.
Because of this result, we can show that 퐹푒preserves bilimits of chains of em-
beddings in 풞. Then, constructing the ﬁxed point of 퐹becomes simply a matter of
adapting the proof of Kleene’s ﬁxed-point theorem.
Theorem 2.2 ([SP82]). Suppose that 풞has bilimits of chains of embeddings. Then
퐹has a minimal invariant 푖∶퐹(퐷, 퐷) ≅퐷.

6


To see how this relates to Pitts’ construction, note that ℛcan also be seen as a
pointed 햢햯햮-category, and the projection 푝∶ℛ→풞preserves this structure. In-
deed, admissibility means that the morphisms of ℛhave the structure of a pointed
CPO inherited from the morphisms of 풞. The terminal object of ℛis just the ter-
minal object of 풞equipped with the greatest relation on ℛ1, which exists because
ℛ1 is a complete lattice. Moreover, if we see each relation 퐹(푅, 푆) as an object of ℛ,
then the action of 퐹on ℛcan be described equivalently as a 햢햯햮-functor of type

To apply the inverse limit construction to this lifted functor, we just need to show
that ℛhas bilimits of chains of embeddings.
Lemma 2.3. Admissible relational structures create bilimits. That is, if 푋is a chain
of embeddings in ℛ, and 푓∶푝푋→∆퐿is a bilimit in 풞, then 푓∶푋→∆푅is a bilimit
in ℛ, where 푅∈ℛ퐿is deﬁned as

Proof. By Theorem 2.1, it suﬃces to show that the corresponding cone of projec-
tions is a limiting cone in ℛ. We’ll show that there is a bijective correspondence

3
Banach Fixed Point
In the last two decades, guarded recursion has emerged as a popular method for
deﬁning recursive relations. While originally developed for reasoning about de-
notational semantics [Nak00], it was shortly after adapted to the operational set-
ting, where it proved to be a convenient interface to step-indexed reasoning [AM01;
App+07].
The basic idea is to work with a family of relations (푅푛)푛∈ℕ. In the case of step
indexing, 푅푛represents a property that holds of terms of a language within at most
푛steps of computation, such as “if the term terminates in at most 푛steps, then it
is a value of type 햻허허헅”. It is always possible to deﬁne such a family recursively if
each 푅푛depends only on the values of 푅푚, for each 푚< 푛. Manipulating such
indices directly quickly becomes cumbersome, so guarded recursion encapsulates
this process in a modality ▹, usually known as “later”. Then, any recursive deﬁni-
tion becomes valid, as long as recursive occurrences of the relation appear under
▹.
After reviewing the basics of guarded recursion, we will see how it leads to an
alternative proof of Theorem 1.1. First, we need a general, abstract setting where
guarded deﬁnitions can be formulated.

on 푋: ⋂

We will soon see examples of OFEs connected to the denotational models we
have been studying so far. Before we get there, however, let us go back to the exam-
ple sketched above: an indexed family of relations on terms 푡∈푇. The set of such
indexed families forms an OFE: we say that 푅
푛= 푆if and only if 푅푚= 푆푚for any
푚< 푛. Intuitively, this means that the two relations are equivalent if we restrict
ourselves to strictly less than 푛steps of computation. To deﬁne ﬁxed points in such
an abstract setting, we require slightly more structure of OFEs.

We can show that the OFE of relations sketched above is complete. Intuitively,
if we look at the 푛th level of the terms of a Cauchy sequence, they will eventually

sequence.
Theorem 3.3 (Banach Fixed Point). Suppose that a function 푓∶푋→푋on a

Remark 3.4 (Connection to metric spaces). Each OFE 푋gives rise to a metric space
as follows: 푑(푥, 푦) = 2−푛, where 푛is the greatest 푛such that 푥

Remark 3.5 (Deﬁning later). Given a family of relations (푅푛)푛∈ℕas above, we can

4
Kleene Fixed Point
As observed earlier, the inverse limit construction can be seen as a generalization
of Kleene’s ﬁxed point theorem:
Theorem 4.1 (Kleene). Let 푋be a pointed CPO and 푓∶푋→푋be a continuous
function. Then 푓has a least ﬁxed point 푥= 푓(푥), given by the limit of the chain
⊥⊑푓(⊥) ⊑푓2(⊥) ⊑⋯.
As a minor variation on Section 2, let us sketch how we can restate those results
using Kleene’s theorem, by viewing domains and relations as an ordered structure
rather than a category. There are two issues that we need to address. First, ℛcon-
tains potentially many morphisms between a pair of objects, whereas a CPO 푋seen
11


as a category has at most one. Second, ℛis not a skeletal category: there are objects
that are isomorphic, but not equal. By contrast, a CPO seen as a category is skeletal
because its order is antisymmetric.
To solve the ﬁrst issue, consider the slice category 풞푒∕퐷. Objects of 풞푒∕퐷are
embeddings of type 푋→퐷, and arrows from 푋→퐷to 푌→퐷commuting trian-
gles of embeddings:
푋
푌
퐷.
Since embeddings are monomorphisms, if there are two arrows of type 푋→푌
in 풞푒∕퐷, they must be equal. We can apply a similar idea to ℛby considering
ℛ(풞푒∕퐷), which is deﬁned as the following pullback:
ℛ(풞푒∕퐷)
ℛ푒
풞푒∕퐷
풞푒
⌟
Explicitly, objects of ℛ(풞푒∕퐷) are triples 푋= (|푋|, 푒푋∶|푋| →퐷, 푅푋∶ℛ푋), where
푒푋is an embedding. An arrow 푓∶푋→푌is an embedding 푓푒∶|푋| →|푌| such
that 푒푌푓푒= 푒푋and such that, in ℛ, we have 푓푒∶푅푋→푅푌and 푓푝∶푅푌→푅푋.
Once again, there is at most one arrow of any given type in this category.
To solve the second issue, note that, in many cases of interest, we can replace
풞푒∕퐷(and ℛ(풞푒∕퐷)) with equivalent skeletal subcategories, by choosing canonical
representatives for their objects. For instance, if 풞is 햢햯햮⊥, we can replace an em-
bedding 푒푋∶푋→퐷with its image in 퐷, which is isomorphic to 푋. Two objects in
풞푒∕퐷are isomorphic if and only if their images in 퐷are equal. In what follows, I’ll
assume that such canonical representatives exist, and that 퐷→퐷is its own repre-
sentative. By abuse of notation, I’ll identify the above categories with their skeletal
equivalents.
Both 풞푒∕퐷and ℛ(풞푒∕퐷) are CPOs: to compute the least upper bound of a chain,
we simply project the chain onto 풞(or ℛ), compute its bilimit, and use its canonical
representativein 풞푒∕퐷(or ℛ(풞푒∕퐷)). Moreover,these CPOs are pointed: their least
elements are (1, ⊥∶1 →퐷) and (1, ⊥∶1 →퐷, ⊤). This leads to the following
alternative proof.
Proof of Theorem 1.1; Kleene ﬁxed point. Let 퐹∶풞표푝×풞→풞be a 햢햯햮-functor. As
we have seen in Section 2, we can view the admissible action of 퐹on ℛas a lifting
퐹ℛ∶ℛ표푝× ℛ→ℛ. These functors give rise to functors 퐹푒∶풞푒→풞푒and 퐹푒
ℛ∶
ℛ푒→ℛ푒that preserve colimits of chains. We have the following commutative
diagram:

By working with canonical representatives, we can view these functors as con-
tinuous functions 푓∶풞푒∕퐷→풞푒∕퐷and 푓ℛ∶ℛ(풞푒∕퐷) →ℛ(풞푒∕퐷), and we
can take their ﬁxed points by Theorem 4.1. By construction, the ﬁxed point of 푓
is just 퐷, and the above diagram implies that 푝(햿헂헑(푓ℛ)) = 햿헂헑(푓) = 퐷, where
푝∶ℛ(풞푒∕퐷) →풞푒∕퐷is the canonical projection. This means, after some un-

Remark 4.2. Most categories used in domain theory have canonical representatives
of embeddings—we can take the image of an embedding, as we have done above,
or we can choose representatives using the axiom of choice. But if images are not
available, there is another option that does not rely on the axiom of choice: to work
with ̄풞, the Karoubi envelope of 풞. This category extends 풞by freely splitting all
idempotent arrows in 풞(that is, arrows 푝∶푋→푋such that 푝푝= 푝). Roughly,
this means that ̄풞contains canonical image objects of all idempotents in 풞. In
particular, we can compute the image of the idempotent 푓푒푓푝determined by an
embedding 푓푒∶푌→푋, which yields a choice of representatives for embeddings.
Moreover, ̄풞(and ̄ℛ) inherit the properties of the original categories that we relied
on to carry the above constructions, so our results still apply.
5
Conclusion
We have just reviewed Pitts’ framework of relational properties of domains [Pit96]
and seen how it relates to other important ﬁxed-point theorems: the inverse limit
construction [SP82], Banach’s ﬁxed-point theorem, and Kleene’s ﬁxed point theorem.
These connections are implicit in some of the existing literature, and probably al-
ready known by experts. For example, the work of Hermida and Jacobs [HJ98]
presents a diﬀerent method for constructing relations on recursive data types that
requires lifting limits and colimits along a ﬁbration; likewise, the proof of Pitts’
method with the inverse limit construction uses Lemma 2.3, which lifts bilimits
to a relational structure. As for Banach’s ﬁxed-point theorem, several works for
reasoning about denotational models [BST09b; BST09a; Bir+11] employ similar
constructions while sometimes noting that Pitts’ framework could have been used
instead [BST09b]. Here, we have seen how this connection goes beyond the con-
struction of a particular set of logical relations, and lies at the heart of Pitts’ method.
It is worth noting that the connections between these ﬁxed-point theorems go be-
yond the setting of relational reasoning—e.g. Thamsborg [Tha10] discusses how
we can view Banach’s ﬁxed-point theorem as an instance of Kleene’s.
Traditionally, step-indexing uses the steps in some operational semantics to de-
ﬁne recursive relations [AM01]. In light of the connections explained above, Pitts’
13


construction—as well as other applications of Banach’s ﬁxed-point theorem for de-
notational models [Bir+11; BST09a; BST09b; MPS86]—use a similar trick to ensure
that the recursion is well-founded, but count the number of unfoldings of a recur-
sive type instead. In this sense, guarded recursion is more general, since the notion
of counting can be tied to anything that can be tracked in the execution of a pro-
gram, not just the number of unfoldings of the domain equation. On the other
hand, relations constructed with Pitts’ method are often cleaner then their guarded
counterparts, because they do not have to mention step indices or guards explicitly.
One question that I have not explored is how this connects to variants of Pitts’
construction used for operational semantics, as developed by Birkedal and Harper
[BH99] or Crary and Harper [CH07]. Such works note that the projections 휋푖can
often be deﬁned as regular programs in a language, and leverage this fact to adapt
Pitts’ ideas to establish powerful reasoning principles forprogramequivalence. Like
Pitts’ original construction, these works employ the Knaster-Tarski ﬁxed-point the-
orem, but I believe that it might be possible to adapt their constructions to leverage
other results as well. One possible connection lies in the proof of metric preser-
vation for the Fuzz language [RP10]. Its argument employed step-indexed logical
relations, but the indices of the relations tracked the number of recursive unfold-
ings reduced during execution rather than the number of transitions in a small-step
semantics. This idea is similar to constructions by guarded recursion performed in
denotational settings [BST09a; BST09b; Bir+11; MPS86], suggesting that it might
be possible to obtain an alternative, operational proof of metric preservation for
Fuzz along the lines of Birkedal and Harper [BH99] and Crary and Harper [CH07].
